html:
REACT Group PLC is a tiny UK facilities-management support services roll-up that the market still prices like a stale COVID cleaning stock. That is the wrong frame. The current business is mostly repeat, essential, time-sensitive work across commercial window cleaning, contract cleaning, specialist/reactive cleaning, and now drainage/plumbing through 24hr Aquaflow. The February 2026 audited FY25 results show revenue up 20% to £24.9m, gross profit up 40% to £8.0m, gross margin up to 32.1%, and adjusted EBITDA up 27% to £3.1m, with management-defined recurring revenue at 93% (FY25 results).

The variant perception is not “specialist cleaner grows a bit.” It is that REACT is slowly becoming a higher-margin urgent-response services platform while the quoted equity still reflects the old narrative: microcap, low-quality cleaning, COVID hangover, acquisition debt, no liquidity. The key line in the FY25 filing is that 24hr Aquaflow operates at close to 50% gross margin, versus 26.1% for the rest of the group. That acquisition is not cosmetic. Drainage failures, blocked interceptors, pump issues, and urgent plumbing problems have a real cost of downtime. They are closer to emergency infrastructure maintenance than janitorial services.

The market is also missing that FY25’s messy free cash flow is not proof that the model broke. Reported FCF was slightly negative, but the cash drag included pre-acquisition Aquaflow tax, old tax settlement, VAT timing, higher interest, leases, and growth capex for Project Sparkle and drainage/pump capability. That matters because the stock is valued as if EBITDA is low-quality. At 46.5p on the AIM line, REACT’s market cap is only £11.0m (quote). With £2.9m reported net debt, EV is about £13.9m, or 4.5x FY25 adjusted EBITDA. Including deferred/contingent consideration, the economic EV is still only about £16.1m, or 5.3x. On a forward base case of £3.4m to £3.7m adjusted EBITDA, the equity is priced at roughly 4x forward EBITDA before deferred consideration, for a business with 30%+ gross margins and repeat/contracted revenue.

The March 31, 2026 AGM update matters because it confirms the inflection did not end with the annual report. Management said trading continued to perform well, sales and marketing initiatives were working across all divisions, the pipeline remained encouraging, Aquaflow was contributing positively, and Project Sparkle had become a clear differentiator with a customer portal underway (AGM statement). The latest May 2026 PDMR/SIP filings are not large enough to be a core signal, but there has been insider participation at roughly 44.8p to 50.6p, not insider abandonment (May PDMR).

The best upside case is operational, not promotional. Project Sparkle should make LaddersFree easier to route, monitor, and scale nationally. Aquaflow adds higher-margin urgent technical work. Cross-selling is now credible because the same FM customer can buy window cleaning, reactive cleaning, contract cleaning, drainage, CCTV inspections, pumps, and urgent callout cover from one small supplier. Recent contract wins across The Works, BP Forecourts, H&M, Homes England, Smart Managed Solutions, and industrial customers show that this is not just management narrative (contract wins).

The social/retail context is useful because there barely is one. The most substantive retail attention I found was a 2025 r/ValueInvesting post calling REACT an ignored AIM nanocap with little sell-side attention and poor IR visibility (Reddit discussion). Since then, the stock has fallen, while gross margin, recurring revenue mix, and adjusted EBITDA have improved. That is exactly the kind of stale-perception gap that can persist in a £11m market-cap name until one or two clean reporting periods force a re-rate.

The bear case is real but not dominant. The company has acquisition leverage, deferred consideration, covenanted HSBC debt, wage pressure, low liquidity, and a history of issuing equity for deals. If FY26 shows that cash conversion remains weak after the temporary items roll off, or that Aquaflow’s high margin cannot scale, the stock deserves to stay cheap. But downside is not “zero unless everything goes right.” REACT has recurring/repeat revenue, improving gross margins, a broader customer base, and term debt that amortizes monthly. The balance sheet is stretched enough to cap reckless M&A, but not stretched enough to overwhelm the equity if EBITDA holds.

My base case is FY26 revenue of £26m to £27m, adjusted EBITDA around £3.5m, and normalized pre-acquisition-payment free cash flow of roughly £1.4m to £1.8m. A modest 6x to 6.5x EBITDA multiple, still a discount to higher-quality support-services peers, supports an equity value near £18m to £20m after debt and deferred consideration. The bull case is £4m+ EBITDA, visible deleveraging, and evidence that Aquaflow plus Project Sparkle can drive organic growth without new equity. At that point, 7.5x to 8.5x EBITDA is not heroic.

Current price: about €0.53 per D1I.F share, using the May 15 AIM close of 46.5p translated to euros. The Frankfurt line is very illiquid, and some quote services show stale prints closer to €0.48, so the AIM line is the cleaner economic reference.

Bear target: €0.30, down 43%. EBITDA disappoints, FCF remains poor, and the market applies a distressed 4x multiple after including acquisition liabilities.

Base target: €0.88, up 66%. FY26 confirms £3.4m to £3.7m EBITDA, cash conversion normalizes, and the stock re-rates to a still-cheap small support-services multiple.

Bull target: €1.35, up 155%. Aquaflow scales, Project Sparkle improves LaddersFree economics, EBITDA clears £4m, debt starts falling, and REACT gets valued as a niche urgent-response FM services platform rather than a failed COVID cleaner.
